explicit_text: I was born in Los Angeles, California, on a sunny Saturday in April. I lived there until I was nine years old before moving to London. It was my first home and shaped my early childhood as a true California kid.

explicit_text: After living in Los Angeles, I moved to London, England when I was nine years old and grew up there until I was 15. Those years in London were formative before I returned to the United States to finish high school. It was quite a change from California to London during my childhood.

explicit_text: When applying to colleges, I was accepted to the United States Naval Academy but ultimately chose Tufts University instead. I was concerned that at the Naval Academy, I might end up in the Navy rather than the Marine Corps, which was my goal. So while the Naval Academy was an option, I pursued a different path to ensure a Marine Corps commission.

I initially thought I wanted to study International Affairs but ended up majoring in history and literature before entering a five-year bachelor's/master's program. Tufts was where I combined my academic interests with military training, graduating in May 2003.

explicit_text: I was commissioned in May 2003, just after the Iraq invasion began in March and April of that year. Throughout my training and early career, the Iraq War shaped the Marine Corps' mission and my assignments. I was preparing to deploy to Iraq during a time of intense counter-insurgency fighting, including in places like Fallujah and Ramadi.

explicit_text: I joined the United States Marine Corps through ROTC at Tufts and was commissioned as a second lieutenant in May 2003. My training included the Basic School and Infantry Officer Course, and I was preparing to deploy to Iraq during a time of significant combat operations. The Marine Corps has been central to my life and career since college.

explicit_text: I checked into the Marine Corps Basic School in June 2003, right after the Iraq invasion. Initially, there was a sense that the war was over, but by the time I graduated that Christmas, it was clear we were heading into intense combat. The school was where I transitioned from student to Marine officer amid a changing war environment.

explicit_text: In August 2003, the Marine Corps Times ran a front-page story titled 'The Marines Back to Iraq,' signaling the Marine Corps' return to combat operations. This story coincided with a shift in attitude among instructors and students at the Basic School about the war's seriousness. The publication captured the changing reality we faced as new officers.

explicit_text: A friend of mine who had deployed to Al Anbar Province in Iraq sent me an email describing the initial quiet patrols there in early 2003. However, that calm was shattered when the Blackwater contractors were killed on the Fallujah bridge, and fighting erupted. Al Anbar was a focal point of the insurgency and where many Marines, including my peers, saw combat.

explicit_text: Paul Felsberg was a friend of mine from the Infantry Officer Course who was a prior enlisted Marine. He graduated early to replace a fallen Marine officer named Reblowski and was killed in Ramadi in October 2004. His loss was a personal reminder of the dangers we faced in Iraq.

explicit_text: I was in college when the September 11 attacks happened, and it changed everything for me and my generation. Before 9/11, my brother thought I’d never see combat, but after the attacks, it became clear that war was likely. The attacks put us all on notice and increased the certainty that I would be deployed to fight.
